synthesis_text: Secure the WebSocket channel by always using wss (TLS) (N1:0) and verifying the Origin header on upgrade (N5:3). Authenticate each connection with a short‑lived signed JWT (or similar token) that includes a per‑connection nonce and expires quickly (N1:1, N3:5); rotate and invalidate tokens on reconnect (N1:7). For every event, embed a monotonically increasing sequence number (N1:2) and a globally unique event ID (UUID) (N1:4). The server records the highest sequence number seen per client and discards any message with a lower or equal number (N1:3), while also keeping a short‑term cache of recent UUIDs to drop duplicates. Protect payload integrity and replay by adding a per‑message HMAC or signed payload (N1:6) and enforcing a replay window of recent sequence numbers (N3:2). When a client reconnects, it sends the last processed sequence number so the server can resume from the next one (N3:3). Optionally, use application‑level ACKs so the client can request retransmission of missing messages (N5:7). This combination ensures ordered delivery, deduplication, and robust token‑based security against replay and tampering.

Secure the WebSocket channel by using wss (TLS) for encryption (N1:0) and authenticating each connection with a short‑lived signed token such as a JWT (N1:1). Rotate tokens on reconnect and invalidate old ones after a brief grace period (N1:7). Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in every event payload per client (N1:2) and have the server track the highest sequence number processed per client, dropping any event with a lower or equal number (N1:3). Optionally embed a timestamp and buffer out‑of‑order events until missing sequence numbers arrive, discarding them after a timeout (N1:5). Attach a unique event ID (UUID) and maintain a recent ID cache (e.g., LRU) to deduplicate retransmissions (N1:4). Protect each message with a per‑message HMAC or signed payload to detect tampering and replay of old messages (N1:6). When a client reconnects, it should send the last sequence number it processed so the server can resume from the next number (N3:3). Define a replay window (e.g., last N sequence numbers) and ignore events outside this window to prevent replay attacks (N3:2).

Secure the WebSocket channel by always using wss (TLS) and verifying the Origin header (N1:0, N5:3).  Authenticate each upgrade with a short‑lived signed JWT that contains a per‑connection nonce and expires quickly (N1:1, N5:4, N3:5).  Rotate or invalidate the token on every reconnect and enforce a short grace period (N1:7).  Protect every message with a per‑message HMAC or signed payload so tampering and replay are detectable (N1:6).  Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in each event (N1:2, N5:0) and have the server keep the highest sequence number seen per client; any event with a lower or equal number is dropped (N1:3).  When a client reconnects it should send the last sequence number it processed so the server can resume from the next number (N3:3).  Attach a globally unique event ID (UUID) to each payload and keep a short‑term LRU cache of recent IDs to filter duplicate retransmissions (N1:4, N5:1).  Optionally embed a timestamp and, if out‑of‑order events arrive, buffer them briefly or discard after a timeout (N1:5).  This combination provides ordering, deduplication, and strong replay‑token protection for real‑time WebSocket updates.

Secure the WebSocket channel by using wss (TLS) for all connections (N1:0) and authenticating each upgrade with a short‑lived signed token such as a JWT (N1:1). Rotate or invalidate tokens on reconnect (N1:7) and bind them to a per‑connection nonce to limit replay. For ordering, embed a monotonically increasing sequence number in every event per client (N1:2) and have the server keep the highest processed number, discarding any event with a lower or equal value (N1:3). To handle duplicate transmissions, also attach a unique event ID (UUID) and maintain a short‑term cache (e.g., LRU) of recent IDs to drop repeats (N1:4). Optionally include a timestamp; if events arrive out‑of‑order, buffer until missing sequence numbers appear or discard after a timeout (N1:5). Protect each message against tampering and replay by adding a per‑message HMAC or signed payload (N1:6). This combination provides encrypted transport, authenticated sessions, deterministic ordering, deduplication, and replay protection.

Secure the WebSocket channel by always using wss (TLS) to encrypt traffic (N1:0) and verify the Origin header (N5:3). Authenticate each upgrade with a short‑lived signed JWT (or similar token) that includes a per‑connection nonce or jti claim (N1:1, N5:4) and rotate or invalidate the token on reconnect (N1:7). Protect every message with a per‑message HMAC or signed payload (N1:6) so tampering and replay of old messages are detectable. Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in each event per client (N1:2) and have the server keep the highest sequence number seen, discarding any event with a lower or equal number (N1:3). Additionally, attach a globally unique event ID (UUID) and maintain a short‑term cache (e.g., LRU) of recent IDs to drop duplicate retransmissions (N1:4). Define a replay window based on recent sequence numbers or timestamps and ignore events outside this window (N3:2). When a client reconnects, it should send the last processed sequence number so the server can resume from the next number (N3:3). Optionally, use timestamps to buffer out‑of‑order events for a limited time before discarding them (N1:5). Implement a sliding window of accepted nonces/timestamps to further guard against replay attacks (N5:6).
